caveats: a: OEWS is only available for NAICS 236100 and excludes self-employed workers.
a: The inference that multifamily firms have a more professional and less craft-heavy internal mix is not directly measured.
a: Task applicability does not imply that entire jobs can be removed, especially where review and liability remain human.
rho: Survey measures combine current use, pilots, scaling, and future investment rather than implemented labor savings.
rho: Grant Thornton combines construction and real estate and discusses owners and property managers as well as contractors.
rho: AGC respondents span construction segments and include firms larger than the target band.
e: The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed EBITDA-band count.
e: NAICS codes do not identify captive contractors, temporary joint ventures, customer concentration, or transferable bonding capacity.
e: Contractors can participate economically in development projects even when classified as third-party builders.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: Construction-services transaction counts have no industry-specific denominator and skew toward other verticals.
s5: Owner intentions and announced transactions do not guarantee qualifying completed control transfers.
q: Contract type is an indirect proxy for discounted benefit retention.
q: The multifamily contract-mix figures come from an advisory article rather than a published representative sample.
q: Actual savings ownership depends on negotiated fee, contingency, allowance, shared-savings, and change-order clauses.
d5: Housing-start units are not a direct measure of contractor service quantity or constant-quality output.
d5: The Census monthly annualized rate is volatile and NAHB's forecast extends explicitly only through 2027.
d5: Both sources include delivery models outside NAICS 236116 and national averages conceal large metro differences.
o: No source directly measures the future share requiring this operator type.
o: Large developers may internalize construction management or create captive entities outside the screened lens.
o: Modular delivery and integrated platforms could shift more coordination away from independent GCs than assumed.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based outsourced construction for developers, investors, housing authorities, and institutional owners, commonly delivered under guaranteed-maximum-price, lump-sum, design-build, or construction-management arrangements; repeatability comes from a continuing pipeline of projects and repeat developer relationships rather than subscription revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: For-sale or merchant builders constructing on their own account, owner-lessors constructing for their own portfolios, residential remodelers, specialty-trade subcontractors, prefabricated-building manufacturers, captive construction units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market U.S. general contractors, design-build firms, and construction-management firms building new multifamily apartments and condominiums for external owners and developers, including high-rise, garden, town-house, low-income, and panelized projects.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupation and wage shares for the broader Residential Building Construction industry, not NAICS 236116. It reports management at 13.05% of employment and business/financial operations at 8.85%, including construction managers, project-management specialists, and cost estimators. O*NET identifies the mix of document, planning, reporting, compliance, contracting, and field-accountability tasks within construction management, while Microsoft measures real-world generative-AI applicability to information and writing activities. I classify office, finance, estimating, document control, scheduling, procurement, reporting, and portions of project management as exposed and weight them toward their higher wages, while keeping field supervision, safety, inspection, negotiation, and physical work mostly unexposed. Because multifamily GCs often subcontract field trades and retain a professional project-control layer, the broad residential mix likely understates exposure; however, its inclusion of for-sale builders and remodelers introduces unknown bias.
caveats: OEWS is only available for NAICS 236100 and excludes self-employed workers.
The inference that multifamily firms have a more professional and less craft-heavy internal mix is not directly measured.
Task applicability does not imply that entire jobs can be removed, especially where review and liability remain human.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupation-by-wage tabulation for NAICS 236116 and time-and-motion data from multifamily GCs separating estimating, BIM/document control, project administration, finance, field supervision, and self-performed craft hours would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Multifamily construction firms coordinate complex designs, budgets, bids, submittals, RFIs, contracts, permits, schedules, pay applications, and owner reporting. Those information-heavy workflows are well aligned with current AI, while the execution system still requires human judgment across site conditions, safety, quality, subcontractors, and regulatory accountability. The result is a substantial but minority share of the wage base exposed to substitution or avoidable hiring.

d5.bridge: NAHB forecasts multifamily starts falling from 413,000 in 2025 to 392,000 in 2026 and 367,000 in 2027, then leveling near pre-pandemic levels; Census reports a volatile June 2026 annualized rate of 513,000 starts in buildings with at least five units. I use the NAHB annual forecast path rather than the monthly spike to infer a central five-year quantity modestly below the current cycle, then widen for financing, vacancies, project size, and regional variation. Unit starts cover all delivery models, including own-account builders, and are not constant-price, constant-quality demand for outsourced NAICS 236116 services. The increasing share of large buildings can make unit counts understate service intensity, while productivity gains can reduce contractor revenue per unit.
caveats: Housing-start units are not a direct measure of contractor service quantity or constant-quality output.
The Census monthly annualized rate is volatile and NAHB's forecast extends explicitly only through 2027.
Both sources include delivery models outside NAICS 236116 and national averages conceal large metro differences.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of constant-dollar multifamily construction put in place that separates outsourced GC work from own-account development, with project pipeline, construction lending, permit, vacancy, absorption, and building-size scenarios, would materially move the range.
d5.rationale: A record wave of completions, elevated vacancies, tight lending, and high construction costs point to a near-term reset, not disappearance of the market. Affordability constraints and continued renter demand support a later floor, but the five-year quantity of new multifamily construction remains highly sensitive to capital markets and local regulation.

caveats: The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate rather than an observed EBITDA-band count.
NAICS codes do not identify captive contractors, temporary joint ventures, customer concentration, or transferable bonding capacity.
Contractors can participate economically in development projects even when classified as third-party builders.
e.change_evidence: A representative census of NAICS 236116 LMM firms showing third-party revenue, developer concentration, backlog, bonding capacity, joint-venture status, owner labor dependence, and audited normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition is centered on outsourced general contracting, design-build, and construction management for multifamily projects, so most firms in the EBITDA band fit the external-service lens. The estimate excludes misclassified own-account developers or lessors, captive entities, project joint ventures without a durable operating company, firms with one-client dependence, and businesses whose licenses, bonding, backlog, or relationships cannot transfer apart from the founder.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 236116 ($4,894,577,277) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 236116 ($67,656,215,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1414 (margins.json[236]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Homebuilding EBITDA/Sales 14.14%); revenue/employee $1,337,793 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 236116); class <5: 2,248 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,011,153 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $142,977 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 534 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,499,110 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $636,174 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 366 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,430,377 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,757,655 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 414 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,593,261 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,285,087 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 101 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,435,297 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,028,151 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $316,066,447 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $44,691,796 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 780

caveats: No source directly measures the future share requiring this operator type.
Large developers may internalize construction management or create captive entities outside the screened lens.
Modular delivery and integrated platforms could shift more coordination away from independent GCs than assumed.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on multifamily projects delivered through owner self-management, vertically integrated developers, modular manufacturers, or software-led direct subcontracting, together with state licensing, bonding, insurance, warranty, and lender requirements, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Almost all surviving multifamily construction quantity should still require an accountable general contractor, design-builder, or construction manager to coordinate many trades, control site safety and quality, obtain permits, manage contracts, resolve field problems, and accept schedule and cost obligations. AI, BIM, prefab, and direct procurement can shrink coordination labor, but they do not replace the legal and operational counterparty for complex occupied-building delivery.

q.bridge: A real-estate finance adviser estimates that GMP contracts represent 60-70% of multifamily deals, lump-sum contracts 20-30%, and uncapped cost-plus less than 5%; AIA's standard large-project form confirms that cost-plus-with-GMP makes the construction manager contractually responsible to finish at or below the maximum price. I map these forms to retention: lump-sum work can retain in-project savings, while open-book GMP work reimburses actual cost plus fee and commonly exposes direct savings to the owner, subject to contingency, fee, and incentive terms. Future competitive bids also reprice savings. The adviser figures are not a representative statistical survey and may overstate institutional deal practices relative to LMM contractors; this makes the direction of error uncertain.
caveats: Contract type is an indirect proxy for discounted benefit retention.
The multifamily contract-mix figures come from an advisory article rather than a published representative sample.
Actual savings ownership depends on negotiated fee, contingency, allowance, shared-savings, and change-order clauses.
q.change_evidence: A contract-level multifamily dataset showing revenue by GMP, lump-sum, cost-plus, and design-build form; ownership of savings and contingency; fee structure; audit rights; and gross-margin changes after workflow automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Multifamily owners, lenders, and auditors usually have more cost transparency and negotiating power than individual homeowners. Contractors can retain benefits embedded in fixed fees, corporate overhead, reduced error risk, faster bid throughput, and capacity, but direct job-cost savings under open-book GMP structures often accrue partly or mostly to the owner and are competed away on later projects.

rho.bridge: AGC's broad construction survey finds 61% of firms use AI or plan greater investment, with 45% using it in office/administrative work, 23% in estimating, and 20% in design or preconstruction. Grant Thornton's construction-and-real-estate survey finds 50% piloting AI and 36% scaling it across functions, but only 13% confident they could pass an AI-governance audit in 90 days. I convert adoption and scaling signals into five-year implementation of the exposed opportunity, discounting pilots, assistive use, governance gaps, data integration, and review requirements. These samples include larger owners, property managers, and contractors, so they likely overstate implementation capability for LMM multifamily GCs, though multifamily's structured project data may offset part of that bias.
caveats: Survey measures combine current use, pilots, scaling, and future investment rather than implemented labor savings.
Grant Thornton combines construction and real estate and discusses owners and property managers as well as contractors.
AGC respondents span construction segments and include firms larger than the target band.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of LMM multifamily GCs reporting production deployment by workflow, integration cost, user adoption, error rates, review time, and realized headcount avoidance would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: The most exposed workflows are already named adoption areas, and multifamily projects generate structured budgets, schedules, models, submittals, and pay-application data that can support implementation. Realized labor substitution will lag tool availability because systems must integrate across owners, architects, subcontractors, lenders, and field teams, with strong audit and approval controls.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures U.S. business owners across industries: 14% of owners primarily working in their businesses expected to sell, go public, or transfer ownership within five years, rising to 17% among owners age 55 or older; 52.3% of employer businesses have owners at least 55. Capstone reports 562 construction-services transactions in 2025, up 18.2%, but most cited activity is in infrastructure and subcontractors rather than multifamily GCs. I anchor near Gallup's five-year intention rate and allow a modest uplift for active construction consolidation and professionalized LMM firms, while excluding closures, minority investments, internal reorganizations, and non-transferable founder-led businesses. Broad construction M&A likely overstates buyer depth for multifamily GCs, while Gallup intentions may miss unplanned sales.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to the LMM EBITDA band.
Construction-services transaction counts have no industry-specific denominator and skew toward other verticals.
Owner intentions and announced transactions do not guarantee qualifying completed control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction database and broker cohort specific to NAICS 236116 firms with $1-10M EBITDA, including completed control sales, family transfers, ESOPs, minority deals, failed processes, and closures, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Aging owners and a visible construction M&A market support a meaningful transfer probability. Multifamily GCs can offer transferable teams, systems, backlog, bonding relationships, and repeat developer accounts, but cyclicality, project concentration, working-capital needs, and contingent liabilities can prevent or delay control sales.

principal_driver: Complex, document-heavy project coordination gives AI a credible role in a high-value outsourced workflow that still requires an accountable contractor.
principal_weakness: Open-book contracting and a financing-sensitive construction cycle limit how much implemented efficiency becomes durable operator profit.

run_meta.title: New Multifamily Housing Construction (except For-Sale Builders)

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 236116 as general contractors constructing new multifamily housing and includes multifamily design-build and construction-management firms; excludes own-account for-sale builders, remodelers, specialty trades, prefabricated-building manufacturers, and owner-lessors.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: Reports management occupations at 120,890 jobs, 13.05% of NAICS 236100 employment, and business/financial operations at 81,990 jobs, 8.85%, including 59,800 construction managers, 36,330 project-management specialists, and 14,600 cost estimators.
sources.title: BLS May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Residential Building Construction

sources.supports: Lists construction-manager tasks including compliance inspection, scheduling, budgets and reports, contracts, plan interpretation, permits, trade oversight, and resolution of site problems.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Construction Managers

sources.supports: Analyzes 200,000 anonymized Copilot conversations and finds the highest AI applicability in knowledge work, including office and administrative support, with common activities in information gathering, writing, providing information, and advising.
sources.title: Working with AI: Measuring the Occupational Implications of Generative AI

sources.supports: Reports 61% of contractor respondents use AI or plan increased investment, including 45% in office and administrative work, 23% in estimating, and 20% in design or preconstruction; the survey had 951 respondents.
sources.title: AGC 2026 Construction Hiring and Business Outlook

sources.supports: Reports 50% of surveyed construction and real-estate leaders are piloting AI, 36% are scaling it across functions, 63% cite efficiency gains, and only 13% are confident of passing an AI-governance audit within 90 days.
sources.title: Grant Thornton: Multifamily AI Growth Starts with Governance

sources.supports: Reports 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses have owners age 55 or older; 14% of working owners planned a sale, public offering, or transfer within five years, rising to 17% among owners at least 55.
sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports 562 announced or closed construction-services transactions in 2025, up 18.2% from 2024; private-equity buyers represented 54.3% of sector transactions.
sources.title: Capstone Partners Construction Services M&A Update

sources.supports: States GMP contracts account for 60-70% of multifamily deals, lump-sum contracts 20-30%, and uncapped cost-plus less than 5%, and describes GMP as open-book with contractor exposure above the maximum.
sources.title: Fident Capital: Understanding Construction Contract Types

sources.supports: Describes a standard large-project agreement where a construction manager provides preconstruction and construction services and becomes bound to complete labor and materials at or below an accepted guaranteed maximum price.
sources.title: AIA A133-2019 Owner and Construction Manager Agreement, Cost Plus with GMP

sources.supports: Reports a June 2026 seasonally adjusted annual rate of 513,000 starts and 413,000 completions in buildings with five units or more.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau Monthly New Residential Construction, June 2026

sources.supports: Forecasts multifamily starts at 413,000 in 2025, 392,000 in 2026, and 367,000 in 2027 before leveling near pre-pandemic rates; reports a 7.3% vacancy rate in December and 608,000 completions in 2024.
sources.title: NAHB: Multifamily Market Expected to Cool in 2026 as Vacancies Rise

title: New Multifamily Housing Construction (except For-Sale Builders)
